All right, so let's do a quick recap of last time. So we learned three ways of extracting data from data frames, right? Using the dot loc brackets, dot ilok brackets, and then just plain brackets. How do these work? So when we use dot loc, we give it a bunch of row labels and column labels.

Each of these, you know the row labels, column labels can either be a single label if you want like just one column or just one row, right? And here we have to pass in the label, right? So if it's a column, we have to give the name of the column. If you want a specific row, we have to give the value of the index corresponding to that row. We can pass in single label, you can pass a list of labels, you can pass in a slice, or you can pass in a boolean mask.
And again, we saw that some of them are more commonly used in rows and some are more.

Common in columns, right? It's typical. If you're using a single label or a list, usually that's what you do for columns. If you're using a slice or a mask, usually that's what you do for rows. But look, it's flexible, right? You can do the same thing for rows and columns. It has kind of predictable behavior.
I look is very similar except the I stands for integers. So instead of passing labels you pass in numbers starting at 0, right? First row is 0, then 1-2 and so on. 1st column is 1/2 and so on and.
It gives you back the corresponding rows and columns based on the numbers that you pass in.

And when we index with brackets that is right without loc or without iloc, then we only pass in one argument like a single argument. And then depending on what that argument is, Pandas will decide, hey, OK, you passed in this kind of argument, I'm going to treat it as a row label or you passed in that a kind of argument, I'm going to treat it as a column label.

Specifically, if you give just a single label or a list of labels, pandas is like, oh OK you mean rows or sorry, you mean columns, right? Because you said, oh OK, I want a single label, then that's probably a column label, list of label. You probably wanted specific columns.
So indexing with just brackets, if you pass in single thing or a list then you get back columns.

If you pass in a slice or a boolean mask, then you get back rows, right? Selected rows and all the columns. Similarly with the column labels you know it gives you the columns that you wanted and then all of the rows.
For Lok and ilok, if you want like all the rows or all the columns and you only want to select one, then you put a colon for whatever you want all of.
